title: Check yourself: content
questions.0.q: You want a sub-heading, but <h2> looks too small to you. What do you do?
questions.0.options.1: Keep <h2> and enlarge it later with CSS
questions.0.options.2:
Use a

and make it bold
questions.0.options.3: Use <h1>, but with a smaller font
questions.0.explain: The heading level is about meaning, not size. If it is a subsection — it is an <h2>, however small it looks. The size is fixed with CSS. The meaning cannot be fixed with anything.
questions.1.q: You make a list of your favourite films. Their order means nothing. Which do you pick?
questions.1.options.1: <ul> — because the order does not matter
questions.1.options.3: Does not matter — both are lists
questions.1.explain: Numbers say "this is first, this is second". If the order is arbitrary, you are lying to the reader. The test is simple: swap two items. If nothing changes — <ul>.
questions.2.q: You forget to close a <strong> halfway down the page. What happens?
questions.2.options.3: Nothing — <strong> does not have to be closed
questions.2.explain: You said "the important part starts here" and never said where it ends. The browser assumes the important part is everything until the end. It does not complain — it just carries on.
questions.3.options.0: Because sometimes it comes out bold
questions.3.options.1: Because it means emphasis — and the program reading aloud pronounces it differently
questions.3.options.2: Because it is an old tag and no longer used
questions.3.options.3: Because it only works inside a
questions.3.explain: The slant is a consequence, not the meaning. If you need italic text without emphasis — that is CSS's job. Otherwise you are lying to everyone who is not looking with their eyes.
